signals: Softens a request or command — makes it sound casual, light, and less demanding. Roughly equivalent to 'just' in English requests.
position: Immediately after the verb in imperatives, or after the subject+verb in questions. Mid-sentence.

examples.en: Can you just help me a moment?
Come here for a second!
Just look at that!

contrast_note: Unlike 'nur', which can restrict or question, 'mal' purely softens the tone of a request.

signals: Grants permission or encourages action — signals that something is fine to do, without hesitation or worry.
position: After the verb or subject+verb combination. Mid-sentence.

examples.en: Feel free to go.
Go ahead and say that out loud.
Help yourself to more — it's fine.

contrast_note: Unlike 'einfach' (resignation/simplification), 'ruhig' gives permission or encouragement.

signals: Expresses resignation, frustration, or simplification — 'just', 'simply'. Signals the speaker is accepting something or cutting through complication.
position: Mid-sentence, typically after the verb. Can appear early in the clause for emphasis.

examples.en: It's just too hard.
He simply won't come.

examples.de: Ich gehe da einfach nicht hin.
examples.en: I'm just not going there.

contrast_note: As a particle, 'einfach' expresses resignation or frustration. As an adjective/adverb it means 'simple/simply' — context distinguishes the two.

signals: Adds urgency, warning, or emphasis to a command or wish — 'whatever you do', 'just', 'for goodness' sake'.
position: After the imperative verb or in the mid-field. Often in combination with a negation.

examples.en: Whatever you do, don't do that!
Just make sure you're on time.
Whatever you do, don't forget it.

contrast_note: 'Bloß' is more urgent and warning-like than 'nur'. Where 'nur' softens, 'bloß' intensifies a concern or prohibition.

signals: Softens a question or statement, adding a gentle or curious tone. Also used in restrictions: 'only', 'just'.
position: Mid-sentence. In questions, appears after the subject or verb.

examples.en: I just wanted to ask quickly.
What on earth is that?
If only it would work out.

contrast_note: 'Nur' in questions expresses mild puzzlement or curiosity. 'Bloß' in similar contexts adds warning or urgency.

signals: Signals shared knowledge or mild reproach — the speaker assumes the listener already knows this, or is reminding them of something obvious.
position: Mid-sentence, typically after the verb. Never in first position as a particle.

examples.en: You already know that.
He really is so nice.
Well, that's interesting.

contrast_note: As a modal particle, 'ja' is unstressed and mid-sentence. As an answer word ('Yes'), it is stressed and sentence-initial.

signals: Contradiction, reassurance, or invitation — challenges a negative assumption, softens a suggestion, or adds a sense of 'go on, why not'.
position: Mid-sentence, after verb or subject. As answer particle, appears alone at the start of a response.

examples.en: Come along, why don't you!
That's not right, is it.
You do know that, don't you.

contrast_note: Doch as an answer particle ('Doch!' in response to a negative claim) is a distinct use — it corrects a false negative: 'Du hast das nicht gemacht.' — 'Doch, habe ich!'

signals: Confirmation, reproach, or 'after all' — signals that something follows logically or was to be expected. Can add a slightly resigned or accusatory tone.
position: Mid-sentence, after the verb or in the mid-field.

examples.en: That can't be right either — as expected.

examples.de: Du hast es auch nicht versucht.
examples.en: You didn't even try.
He was there too, after all.

contrast_note: As a particle, 'auch' has a flavour of 'what did you expect?' or confirmation. As a regular adverb it simply means 'also' or 'too'.

signals: Adds curiosity, interest, or mild challenge to questions — 'then', '(on earth)'. Makes a question sound conversational rather than interrogative.
position: In questions only. Appears in the mid-field, after the verb.

examples.en: What are you doing here, then?
So how are you doing?
What on earth happened?

contrast_note: 'Denn' only appears in questions — never in statements. As a conjunction it means 'because', which is a different word entirely.

signals: Expresses concern, disbelief, or unease in yes/no questions — signals the speaker hopes the answer is 'no'.
position: In yes/no questions, typically in the mid-field.

examples.en: You're not sick, are you?
Don't tell me he forgot that.
She's not coming, is she?

contrast_note: 'Etwa' in questions signals the speaker fears the answer is yes. 'Denn' in questions adds neutral curiosity — no concern implied.

signals: Expresses probability, supposition, or hedged certainty — 'probably', 'I suppose', 'I would think'.
position: Mid-sentence, typically after the verb or subject+auxiliary. Frequently after 'ist', 'hat', 'wird'.

examples.en: He's probably still on his way.
That's probably right.
She probably doesn't have time.

contrast_note: 'Wohl' hedges — it signals the speaker is guessing. 'Eigentlich' redirects or expresses reservation. 'Schon' reassures.

signals: Reassurance or concession — 'it'll be fine', 'I know, but…'. Acknowledges a concern while suggesting it's manageable.
position: Mid-sentence, after the verb. Also used at the start of a concessive response.

examples.en: It'll work out, don't worry.

examples.de: Schon, aber das ist nicht der Punkt.
examples.en: Fair enough, but that's not the point.
He'll come eventually — give him time.

contrast_note: As a particle, 'schon' reassures. As an adverb it means 'already'. The particle is unstressed; the adverb is stressed.

signals: Reservation, redirection, or 'actually' — signals the speaker is stepping back to reconsider, or gently changing the subject.
position: Mid-sentence. Can appear early in the clause for a more pronounced redirection.

examples.en: What do you actually want?
I should actually be asleep already.

examples.de: Das ist eigentlich nicht so schwer.
examples.en: That's actually not that hard.

contrast_note: 'Eigentlich' redirects or introduces reservation. 'Wohl' expresses probability. 'Schon' reassures — very different flavours.

signals: Inevitability or resignation — 'that's just how it is', 'nothing to be done about it'. Slightly more formal or standard-German register.
position: Mid-sentence, typically after the verb.

examples.en: That's just the way it is.

examples.de: Er kann eben nicht pünktlich sein.
examples.en: He's just incapable of being on time.

examples.de: Das Leben ist eben kein Wunschkonzert.
examples.en: Life is simply not a wish-concert.

contrast_note: 'Eben' and 'halt' are near-synonyms. 'Eben' is standard German and slightly more formal; 'halt' is colloquial and typical of southern Germany and Austria. See also: halt.

signals: Inevitability or resignation — 'that's just how it is', 'what can you do'. Colloquial register, common in southern Germany and Austria.
position: Mid-sentence, typically after the verb. Interchangeable with 'eben' in most contexts.

examples.en: It just won't work — what can you do.

examples.de: Das Leben ist halt schwer manchmal.
examples.en: Life is just hard sometimes.
I'm just like that — can't help it.

contrast_note: 'Halt' and 'eben' are near-synonyms meaning resignation/inevitability. 'Halt' is colloquial and more regional (south); 'eben' is standard German and slightly more formal. See also: eben.

signals: Intensifies negation — 'not at all', 'absolutely not'. Strengthens a negative statement.
position: Directly before 'nicht' or 'kein' (gar nicht, gar kein). Always paired with negation.

examples.en: That's not true at all.
I have absolutely no time.

examples.de: Er hat sich gar nicht gemeldet.
examples.en: He didn't get in touch at all.

contrast_note: 'Gar' only intensifies negation. It cannot stand alone before a positive statement. Do not confuse with 'gar' as an adjective meaning 'cooked/done'.

signals: Genuine emphasis — 'really', 'truly', 'seriously'. Signals that the speaker means exactly what they are saying.
position: Mid-sentence. Can appear before adjectives/adverbs or after the verb.

examples.en: I'm being really serious about this.
That was really good.
Did you really say that?

contrast_note: 'Wirklich' provides genuine emphasis. 'Eigentlich' redirects or questions — they have very different flavours despite both being common.

signals: Marks surprising inclusion — 'even'. Highlights something unexpected as part of a group or outcome.
position: Before the element being highlighted — before a noun, pronoun, verb, or clause.

examples.en: Even he managed it.
She even speaks Chinese.

examples.de: Das kostet sogar weniger als erwartet.
examples.en: It costs even less than expected.

contrast_note: 'Sogar' marks a surprising element. 'Auch' confirms something was expected. They cannot substitute for each other in the same sentence.

signals: Restriction ('only') or temporal deferral ('not until'). Can signal that something is less than expected, or that it hasn't happened yet.
position: Before the restricted element (a number, time, or noun). Can appear mid-sentence.

examples.en: I'm only 20.
We won't be there until 9.

examples.de: Das hab ich erst gestern erfahren.
examples.en: I only found out yesterday.

contrast_note: 'Erst' restricts downward (less than expected) or defers ('not until'). 'Schon' can mean 'already' — the two are near-opposites temporally.

signals: 'Still', 'yet', or 'another/more'. Signals continuation of a state, or that something additional is desired.
position: Mid-sentence, before the element it modifies.

examples.en: He's still asleep.
Not yet.
Another coffee?

contrast_note: 'Noch' continuing or requesting more. 'Erst' restricts — they often form a pair ('erst … noch nicht').

signals: Introduces an explanation or reason — 'you see', 'the thing is', 'because'. Sits inside the clause, not at the start.
position: Mid-sentence, in the mid-field after the verb. Cannot begin a clause (unlike 'weil').

examples.de: Ich kann nicht kommen, ich bin nämlich krank.
examples.en: I can't come — I'm sick, you see.
The thing is, he doesn't have time.

examples.de: Das ist nämlich nicht so einfach.
examples.en: It's not that simple, you see.

contrast_note: 'Nämlich' gives a reason mid-clause. 'Allerdings' concedes a point. They serve opposite rhetorical moves.

signals: Concession or contrast — 'however', 'admittedly', 'though'. Signals that something needs to be qualified.
position: At the start of a clause (causing verb-second inversion), or mid-sentence in the mid-field.

examples.en: That is, however, difficult.

examples.de: Ich komme gerne, allerdings etwas später.
examples.en: I'll be happy to come, though a bit later.

examples.de: Allerdings muss ich vorher noch etwas erledigen.
examples.en: I do, however, need to sort something out first.

contrast_note: 'Allerdings' concedes a drawback. 'Immerhin' concedes a positive. 'Zwar' sets up a concession that 'aber' then counters.

signals: Sets up a concession — 'admittedly', 'true, but…'. Always pairs with 'aber' or a similar counter.
position: Mid-sentence, in the mid-field. The paired counter ('aber') follows later in the same sentence or the next.

examples.de: Ich habe zwar Hunger, aber kein Geld.
examples.en: I'm admittedly hungry, but I have no money.

examples.de: Das ist zwar schön, aber zu teuer.
examples.en: It's nice, admittedly, but too expensive.

examples.de: Er kann zwar gut kochen, aber er will es nicht.
examples.en: He can cook well enough, but he won't.

contrast_note: 'Zwar' always signals 'yes, but' — it needs a counter. 'Allerdings' can stand alone. Do not use 'zwar' without a subsequent 'aber' or 'doch'.

signals: Consolation — 'at least', 'after all'. Acknowledges that something is less than ideal, but highlights a positive aspect.
position: At the start of a clause or in the mid-field.

examples.en: At least he tried.
That's at least a start.
After all, it didn't rain.

contrast_note: 'Immerhin' consoles — it finds a positive in a bad situation. 'Wenigstens' is similar but slightly more resigned. 'Allerdings' concedes a negative.

signals: Wraps up or concedes regardless of other factors — 'in any case', 'at any rate', 'anyway'.
position: Often at the start of a clause (causing inversion) or mid-sentence.

examples.en: That was very interesting, at any rate.
In any case, I have to go now.

examples.de: Ich weiß nicht, was er will — jedenfalls nicht das.
examples.en: I don't know what he wants — certainly not that.

contrast_note: 'Jedenfalls' concludes or sets aside ambiguity. 'Ohnehin'/'Sowieso' signal something would have happened regardless.

signals: Something would happen or be true regardless — 'anyway', 'in any event'. Slightly more formal than 'sowieso'.
position: Mid-sentence, in the mid-field.

examples.de: Das wäre ohnehin zu spät gewesen.
examples.en: That would have been too late anyway.
He didn't want to come anyway.
That's obvious regardless.

contrast_note: 'Ohnehin' and 'sowieso' are synonyms. 'Ohnehin' is slightly more formal, common in written German. 'Sowieso' is more colloquial and spoken.

signals: Something would happen or be true regardless — 'anyway', 'in any case'. More colloquial than 'ohnehin'.
position: Mid-sentence, in the mid-field. Also common at the end of a sentence in speech.

examples.en: I was going there anyway.
It won't work out anyway.
He would have said no regardless.

contrast_note: 'Sowieso' and 'ohnehin' are synonyms. 'Sowieso' is colloquial and common in speech; 'ohnehin' is more formal and written.

signals: Ironic or frustrated emphasis — 'of all people', 'of all things'. Signals that the element highlighted is the most surprising or unwelcome choice.
position: Immediately before the element being highlighted.

examples.en: Of all people, he has to say that.

examples.de: Ausgerechnet heute fällt der Zug aus.
examples.en: Of all days, the train is cancelled today.

examples.de: Ausgerechnet das Teuerste hat er bestellt.
examples.en: Of all things, he ordered the most expensive one.

contrast_note: 'Ausgerechnet' marks ironic or unwelcome coincidence. 'Sogar' marks surprising but positive inclusion. They signal opposite emotional registers.

signals: 'At all', 'in general', or scope-shifting. Can broaden a question/statement to challenge the premise, or intensify a negation.
position: Mid-sentence. Flexible position depending on what it modifies.

examples.en: Do you actually want that at all?
That's absolutely no problem at all.
Did you even read that?

contrast_note: 'Überhaupt' in questions challenges whether the premise holds. 'Eigentlich' redirects to the real point. Different rhetorical moves.

signals: 'In turn', 'on the other hand', 'for their part'. Signals a counterpoint or a logical turn that follows from the previous.
position: Mid-sentence in the mid-field, or at the start of a clause with verb-second inversion.

examples.en: That, in turn, doesn't surprise me.

examples.de: Er war müde, sie wiederum war voller Energie.
examples.en: He was tired; she, on the other hand, was full of energy.

examples.de: Das führt wiederum zu einem anderen Problem.
examples.en: That in turn leads to another problem.

contrast_note: 'Wiederum' signals a logical turn or contrast. 'Allerdings' concedes a qualification. 'Zwar' sets up a two-part concession.

signals: 'At least' — finds a consolation in a disappointing situation. Slightly more resigned than 'immerhin'.
position: Mid-sentence or at the start of a clause.

examples.en: At least he got in touch.

examples.de: Ich kann es nicht ändern, aber wenigstens weiß ich es jetzt.
examples.en: I can't change it, but at least I know now.
At least nobody got hurt.

contrast_note: 'Wenigstens' and 'immerhin' both mean 'at least'. 'Wenigstens' is slightly more resigned; 'immerhin' often sounds more like 'after all, let's be fair'. 'Zumindest' is more neutral/formal.

signals: 'At least' — neutral or formal register. Sets a minimum standard or concession without the resigned tone of 'wenigstens'.
position: Mid-sentence or at the start of a clause.

examples.en: That was at least honest.
At least he made an attempt.
That should at least be possible.

contrast_note: 'Zumindest' is neutral/formal. 'Wenigstens' is slightly resigned. 'Immerhin' highlights a genuine positive. All three mean 'at least' but differ in register and tone.

signals: 'Already' — more formal and written than 'schon'. Signals something has happened sooner than expected or before a reference point.
position: Mid-sentence, in the mid-field before the main verb or before a time/quantity phrase.

examples.en: He has already taken care of everything.
That was already known.

examples.de: Bereits um 6 Uhr war sie wach.
examples.en: She was already awake at 6 o'clock.

contrast_note: 'Bereits' and 'schon' both mean 'already'. 'Bereits' is more formal and common in writing; 'schon' is universal in speech. In formal writing, 'bereits' is generally preferred.

signals: 'Of course', 'certainly', 'admittedly' — formal or regional (Austrian/Bavarian). Can concede or affirm.
position: Often at the start of a clause, or mid-sentence. Causes verb-second inversion when clause-initial.

examples.de: Das ist freilich eine andere Frage.
examples.en: That is, of course, a different question.
Of course he's right.

examples.de: Das Projekt ist freilich nicht einfach.
examples.en: The project is admittedly not easy.

contrast_note: 'Freilich' is formal or regional (Austrian/Bavarian). In standard German the same meaning is covered by 'natürlich' (affirmation) or 'allerdings' (concession). Rare in everyday northern German speech.
